text: Another thing about me is that I have worked in the journalism tech industry for the better
part of a decade before moving on to working on open social protocols, and part of my journey
there was actually seeing the things that reporters and news organizations were going
through and realizing that some of the change that I wanted to affect could not be done
solely by working with journalism institutions.
And so I ended up moving upstream into working in tech companies and social media because
that sort of fundamental substrate ended up being so important for the foundation for
where the publishing industry and journalists have gone.
And in that span of time have linked up and met with some fascinating folks who are thinking
about what this next era of journalism creation looks like and how people are connecting with
communities.
So I'm very happy to have with us today Justin Bank and Lauren Sack.
Justin is the co-founder of Journalism Atlas and if you saw his talk earlier this morning
it was awesome.
Also iterates very quickly.
It was great to see a slide of Aaron who had given a talk immediately beforehand in there
too.
We can just do things.
It's great.
And Journalism Atlas is a wayfinder for the future of news media designed to connect audiences
with independent creators who are telling the stories they choose with purpose for the communities
they cultivate and serve.
Which sounds very well aligned with the things that we talk about here at this conference
and in this community.
And he's also previously led digital initiatives at the Washington Post, NPR, and the New York
Times.
So it's been fun chatting with you about where the industry has come from and where it could
potentially go.
And Lauren is one of the co-founders of Local News International, which you can find on YouTube,
Tiktok, and Instagram, many fun places.
And was also deputy head of video at the Washington Post and oversaw the posts of Tiktok and YouTube
in an early phase of news organizations really grappling with these media.
So, and you also have a background at PBS and looking at digital things there as well.
So very excited to have you both here.
Are there any things that you all want to open with?
I'm going to actually toss it to you really quickly.
Yeah, thanks, Ted.
I guess I'll just quickly say, I think I see some faces from when I was here earlier this
morning, but just to kind of reorient anybody who wasn't here.
Hi, my name is Justin.
I'm very, very happy to be here.
I am a journalist by training, worked in newsrooms for 20 years, actually, more than 20 years
now, kind of creeps up on you.
It's been amazing to be working on these newer initiatives, thinking about independent journalism
at this moment in time when there are so many institutions which have shrunk or under
distress.
That was a lot of the theme of my talk this morning.
A thing that I'm really excited to talk about next to my friend Lauren is what it means for
all the journalists who are making journalism, as well as anyone who does not identify as
a journalist but is creating journalism or high-fidelity information.
And Aaron's talk this morning wonderfully sets this up.
How do you support people who are trying to create high-quality news in the modern era?
I have found myself in the last few months and years gravitating towards protocol-based
thinking.
Really excited to be here and connect with other creators, builders, doers, operators, thinkers
who want to create that world.
And I'm so glad Lauren's here because of her crazy journey that is six, seven months in
of making an independent media business out of a legacy place and all the stuff she is
learning in real time every day, every week, right?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Thank you all for having me.
Delighted to be here.
And thank you for the introduction.
We've been at it at Local News International since August when we took buyouts from the
Washington Post after creating quite a large audience on TikTok and YouTube around a creator
named Dave Jorgensen.
If you're familiar with him, he's the more familiar face of Local News International than
I am.
My career has been spent at traditional organizations, PBS and The Post and the Smithsonian before that,
begging them to think about new audiences and developing content for them around audiences
that probably aren't interacting with them in any other way.
And we decided to go independent just because of a frustration and a sadness that these organizations
are not ready to support these new endeavors.
Yeah.
And I think that that's an amazing segue because what I really am interested in hearing about
is really the mindset of journalism creators who are thinking about this particular moment
in an era where, you know, publishing has certainly changed with the internet and successive generations
of technology and where audiences are, that has really shifted the landscape of what publishing is
and also how that serves journalism creators and people who are doing out both reporting news
but also bringing it to audiences.
And that business dynamic, I think, is one of the most rapidly changing things.
And as people are talking about products, new products could be built, ways that creators
and audiences connect, thinking about that reality of where these businesses are now and the needs
and incentives that they've got as we think about other types of futures, I think is really critical.
And so I'm super curious, actually, about the story of how y'all launch and the way
that you're thinking about both the content that you create and the way that you want to connect
with the audiences that you're serving.
Yeah, we launched knowing that we would have to put together sort of a multifaceted business
and that has proven true even though we're still quite new at this.
But the opportunity really comes from the way that The Post wasn't able to support us in this way
and that we can be multifaceted.
We can bring in sponsorships.
We can monetize our audience directly.
We can sell merchandise to them directly.
We can seek philanthropic support.
We have a consulting piece of our business as well.
So it really is sort of thinking broadly about how we can ignite this audience that we have built
that has this kind of parasocial relationship with our journalist.
And they want to support him and his work.
And we're able to do that in a much more meaningful way now that we're independent.
And Justin, have you seen that as a pattern with other creators as well as you speak with people about the Journalism Atlas
and the way that they're kind of going out in the world?
Yeah, wildly so.
One of the reasons I'm here.
You know, this morning I told the anecdote of being at The Washington Post in 2011
when the Edward Snowden story breaks and all of the interesting parts about the air-gapped computer,
about Bart Gellman coming to the post, of Marty Baron making sense of how we would, you know,
get the story out there of us having to self-aggregate to win audiences back from Google as the aggregators beat us.
And then I told the story about Marisa Kavis in the last 18 months breaking scoops like crazy on Blue Sky
and creating this new version of a funnel, right?
Those are two very clean examples of an individual creator-journalist from a bygone era at this point 15 years ago
having a brand to protect them and yet still fighting to figure out the fidelity information in relation to the audience.
And then Marisa's more recent example.
As we can't, as I said before, if I speak about this, what I find even more is I'm listening to this like crazy.
I mean, Lauren launched six, seven months ago, and I still can't stop asking you questions about how you build your business.
If you go to journalismatlas.com, we have a certain taxonomy of here's these creators,
here's the platforms they're on, here's their geographies, here's their topics, here's their subtopics.
We have other kind of prototypes we're putting in front of funders, putting in front of philanthropists,
putting in front of publishers to say, look at these patterns.
You know, actually, we're going to be in Chicago at ONA, the Online News Association Conference, tomorrow, yes?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
And I think we're going to release this thing if we get the final, go ahead.
Where we went in Chicago, working on behalf of the philanthropist, the philanthropic group press forward to say, let's map your ecosystem.
The amount of, like, ethnic groups, of, like, immigration communities who have these creators on TikTok,
who then have private WhatsApp groups, was a thing we found there.
We're still doing research.
I don't know if we're going to have it all wrapped up in time for Monday or not.
But all the ways in which there's different models that are based on different platforms that, like what Lawrence said,
of, like, we know we're going to be here to reach people.
We know we can be here to monetize people.
We know we can be here to do a version of R&D, of trying to, you know, rough out where else we'll kind of reach people at.
It's an incredible series of portfolios of what news creators are doing to build, to reach, and to monetize.
I think that that's such an interesting segue to a sort of broader topic about when you talk often about software platforms and protocols and things like that,
there's a lot of, like, discussion about, oh, well, we just need people to exclusively use our platform to switch from some other platform to our platform.
And once you go up a strata from that and you're thinking about people who are community organizers or community leaders,
their strategies around how they're connecting with audiences, the affordances different tools give them,
I think is a really important thing for people to think about.
And I'm really curious about how, say, local news international is thinking about their presence
and where you feel you need to be and where you would like to be.
Yeah.
The notion of exclusivity, I think, makes a lot of sense for most industries, but maybe not for the news, for a couple of reasons.
First of all, philosophically, we believe we want everyone to see our content.
So we want to put it in the broadest possible places we can because we want to be sort of democratic in that way.
We know, though, that we can monetize people in different ways because of that.
We sort of want to reach the broadest audience, but ultimately we want to build fans over time.
That's kind of been the goal Dave and I had in mind since we launched in 2019 is, like, do the news, build fans,
and then you can do something with those fans.
So you can monetize those fans, but ultimately we want to deliver the news in any way, in any place where the audience is.
It doesn't mean we need to be everywhere, but we need to be sort of in the broadest possible audience grab.
It's classic top of funnel, I guess, traditionally speaking.
Yeah, I think the only thing I'll, I mean, again, I could listen to Lauren talk about this forever.
It is endlessly fascinating with me to think about people who every day wake up and say, I'm going to think about the news.
What stories are in our pocket?
What have we been looking for?
What are the longer term stories?
I think especially this community, especially, again, just reflecting on Aaron's amazing, you know, speech this morning,
you know, hearing the conversations that kind of have been going on in this room,
talking about everything from micropayments to extraction to what is the purpose of a platform.
You know, in this time and space, you know, in March of 2026, we're at proto is,
and what I've been hearing these last couple of days, stuff like the leaflet crew,
thinking through, like, how do we kind of, like, graph onto something here to torture a word, I guess.
News flows all day, every day.
It just, you know, Lauren wakes up every day and has a million ideas.
She's so good with Dave.
You guys figure out, we'll do this, we'll do this, we'll do this.
And if we want to get to this, that's actually a four-day thing.
We'll kind of get there.
That's in the guts of a news person.
As I think about the builders, as I think about the community organizers,
I think about people who are thinking about paid feeds or the other ideas we kind of heard around here.
The opportunity to work with news creators who are independently trying to do anything
and to grab the daily conversation and port them over, like Lauren's saying,
to a community that you can turn to fans to do something, I mean, it's wildly exciting.
I'm so curious, actually, on that note.
Are there things that you feel unconstrained about or approaches that you can take now
that you feel like you couldn't take?
And we don't necessarily have to personalize it that much.
But I think about the difference between being supported by a publisher and being within a publisher
versus other strategies that you can take once you're independent as a creator.
Because some of this is like being able to take novel approaches and connect with audiences
in ways that, you know, aren't like read the front page of a newspaper or even the concept of a newspaper.
For sure.
And it's mostly format-driven at this point.
Again, we've only been at this for eight months.
But I think we were very boxed into the format we could perform in at The Post for various reasons.
Everyone's worked for a big company and you're on your team and you do what you do.
Now we can make anything for any platform in any format, any length, any tone we want.
And if it doesn't work, then tomorrow we'll change course and we'll try something different.
So that's why we're really excited.
We'll have long form right now.
We operate strictly in short form.
We publish five days a week.
Long form is coming soon, which was something that, like, to do that at The Post would have taken probably three years
and 15 different teams and, you know, a lot of hemming and hawing and disagreeing.
So we're just going to do it.
And if people in three months don't like it, then we'll pivot something different.
We have a newsletter, which, again, was something, like, we could not have done at The Post.
And we just, heck, why not start a newsletter?
So I think flexibility is the main difference.
I'm so curious about if you have a particular framework that you use to think about experimentation.
One of the reasons why I've been really excited about the AppRoto community is the speed of iteration
and being able to try new things that opens up new opportunities.
But then the question is, I kind of have always joked that journalists are actually kind of like many VCs,
but they invest with their time, right?
And so journalists are very willing to go very deep on something they have strong confidence in.
But otherwise, catching their attention is very difficult.
And so, like, you know, you can say that you have investments in current platforms,
but with the opportunities afforded you, are you guys looking for particular types of connections with audience
or a certain size of audience and things like that?
Size, no.
I would say connection, absolutely.
We're looking to build this sort of loyal fan, as I mentioned before.
So that takes time, right?
That's not going to happen overnight.
That's not going to happen if, hey, everyone loves a viral hit.
We love something that goes viral, but ultimately we want to build a fan that's going to subscribe
and then buy a T-shirt and then leave a comment and then maybe monetize in some other kind of way.
And that, you know, gosh, I'll pick a random time, not random, but six months, let's say.
I think that's a fair time, maybe even longer, maybe a year to try something to really sort of see if you can get traction
and build fans that will come back week to week, day to day, and sort of stick with you.
I think it takes a while.
Actually, would you talk about, you know, hearing Ted's question, you know, I'm obviously a fan of L&I
and a long-time consumer from before we worked together to when we worked together to whatever we are now.
Can you talk a little bit about Dave's coverage of young men in the United States
and just kind of the success he's had?
Yeah.
So our audience is predominantly young men across every platform.
We publish right now on YouTube, TikTok, Beehive, Instagram, LinkedIn, and Facebook, and Snapchat.
And across the big three, let's call it YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, the audience is entirely male and not entirely, I'm sorry,
mostly male and largely under the age of 35,
which is an audience that is attracted to a lot of folk right now for a lot of different reasons.
And what we have found about them really is that they are deeply, deeply engaged with the news,
which is something that a lot of research has proven otherwise.
And that's because we use comedy, for the most part, when appropriate, to tell the news.
It's a lot like when I grew up with The Daily Show that folks said they wanted to interact with The Daily Show,
but they weren't interested in getting their news from any other traditional method.
We're seeing that quite a lot now with Dave's fans.
And the way that they interact with him and engage with his content is so thoughtful and so deep that it's pretty surprising.
We use our newsletter a lot to get email response to topics in the news to do deeper analysis than we're able to do in short form video.
And we saw this in a particularly meaningful way after the murder of Charlie Kirk.
We've seen it again around topics around this word patriotism.
We've seen it again recently around Louis Theroux's documentary on the Manosphere,
where we've engaged our audience in this,
and they have written these lengthy, thoughtful, surprising emails back to Dave
about the way that they think about politics, about how they think about their worldview.
And we've really seen that as sort of the foundation of kind of our philosophy of how we deliver the news.
If I can pitch in this room, because it's been so inspiring to be here,
and I love the goose in that we can do things.
God, I want to believe that.
Like, now's the time.

understanding who I'm talking to.
